sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presents with a sore on his right foot that has not been healing for 2-3 weeks.
He initially thought it was a blister from a new pair of shoes, but it has developed into a wound.
He has been using a band-aid to cover it and has been putting it off, but it has not improved despite finishing a course of antibiotics prescribed by his primary care physician.
He reports no pain or sensation in the wound and no other symptoms such as fever, chills, or drainage.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with a last HbA1c of over 8%.
He has a history of neuropathy.
Review of systems: None mentioned.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1°F, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.
Physical exam: There is a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
Bony exposure is not present.
Palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses are present, but no palpable DP or PT pulses are present.
Doppler signs are present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with a possible compromised blood supply, which is a risk factor for delayed healing.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Compromised blood supply; Diabetes mellitus (status: active).
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily and covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I will order an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to assess blood supply to the foot.
I will also refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates suboptimal blood supply for wound healing.
The patient will wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in 2 weeks to reassess the wound and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
